From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Q Magazine](/w/index.php?title=Q_Magazine&redirect=no "Q Magazine"))

British music magazine and online publication

*Q*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Special commemorative issue (September 2020) | |
| Categories | [Music magazine](/wiki/Music_magazine "Music magazine") |
| Frequency | Monthly |
| [Circulation](/wiki/List_of_magazines_by_circulation "List of magazines by circulation") | 44,050 (ABC Jul – Dec 2015)[[1]](#cite_note-1) Print and digital editions. |
| Publisher | [Bauer Media Group](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Group "Bauer Media Group") |
| First issue | October 1986 |
| Final issue | July 2020 |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Based in | London |
| Language | English |
| Website | [qthemusic.com](http://qthemusic.com) |
| [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") | [0955-4955](https://www.worldcat.org/search?fq=x0:jrnl&q=n2:0955-4955) |

***Q*** was a British [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") magazine. It was founded in 1986 by broadcast journalists [Mark Ellen](/wiki/Mark_Ellen "Mark Ellen") and [David Hepworth](/wiki/David_Hepworth "David Hepworth"), who were presenters of the BBC television music series *[The Old Grey Whistle Test](/wiki/The_Old_Grey_Whistle_Test "The Old Grey Whistle Test")*.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) *Q* was published in print in the UK from 1986 until July 2020. In 2023, it was revived as an [online publication](/wiki/Webzine "Webzine"), which ran until May 2024.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

*Q* was originally published by the [EMAP](/wiki/EMAP "EMAP") media group and set itself apart from much of the other music press with monthly production and higher standards of photography and printing.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) In the early years, the magazine was sub-titled "The modern guide to music and more". Originally it was to be called *Cue* (as in the sense of cueing a record, ready to play), but the name was changed so that it would not be mistaken for a [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker") magazine. Another reason, cited in *Q*'s 200th edition, is that a single-letter title would be more prominent on newsstands.

In January 2008, EMAP sold its consumer magazine titles, including *Q*, to the [Bauer Media Group](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Group "Bauer Media Group").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Bauer put the title up for sale in 2020, alongside *Car Mechanic*, *Modern Classics*, *Your Horse*, and *Sea Angler*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Publication ceased in July 2020 as [Kelsey Media](/wiki/Kelsey_Media "Kelsey Media") decided to buy a number of non-music titles from Bauer (*Sea Angler*, *Car Mechanics* and *Your Horse*),[[9]](#cite_note-9) making the 28 July 2020 issue (Q415) the last to be published.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The end of the print version of *Q* was blamed both on lower circulation and advertising revenue caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), as well as being "a symptom of an expert-free internet age."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

After the sale of the brand to Empire Media Group,[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) *Q* was soft launched as an online publication in November 2023, posting new content along with articles from their archive.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was officially relaunched in January 2024, with a new editorial team spearheaded by US Editor Andrew Barker in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and Oxford-based UK and Europe Editor, Dominic Utton.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) In May 2024 the magazine ceased operations. Six full-time journalists were laid off.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Original print magazine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Original print magazine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Q_magazine_first_issue_October_1986.png/250px-Q_magazine_first_issue_October_1986.png)](/wiki/File:Q_magazine_first_issue_October_1986.png)

First issue of *Q* (October 1986)

The magazine had an extensive review section, featuring [new releases](/wiki/Music_release "Music release"), [reissues](/wiki/Reissue "Reissue"), compilations, film and live concert reviews, as well as radio and television reviews. Each review included a rating from one to five stars. While its content was non-free they hosted an archive of all of their magazine covers.[[15]](#cite_note-magazine-covers-15)

Much of the magazine was devoted to interviews with popular music artists.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) According to [Alexis Petridis](/wiki/Alexis_Petridis "Alexis Petridis") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, it was originally set up after the success of "rock’s old stagers" at [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid"), which co-founders Mark Ellen and David Hepworth had co-presented, to focus on long-established acts that appealed to an older music market, such as [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart"), [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon"), [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), [Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_(band) "Genesis (band)"), and [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton").[[16]](#cite_note-petridis-16) It also compiled lists, ranging from "The 100 Greatest Albums" to "The 100 Richest Stars in Rock", with a special edition magazine called "The 150 Greatest Rock Lists Ever" published in July 2004.[[17]](#cite_note-17) *Q* also produced a number of special editions devoted to a single act/artist like [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") or [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), but these magazines stopped in 2018, with its sister magazine, *[Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(magazine) "Mojo (magazine)")*[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) (also owned by Bauer) continuing to produce specials devoted to artists like [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan").

Promotional gifts were given away, such as cover-mounted [CDs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc")[[15]](#cite_note-magazine-covers-15) or books. The January 2006 issue included a free copy of "The Greatest Rock and Pop Miscellany … Ever!", modelled on *[Schott's Original Miscellany](/wiki/Schott%27s_Original_Miscellany "Schott's Original Miscellany")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Each issue of *Q* had a different message on the spine. Some readers tried to work out what the message had to do with the contents of the magazine. This practice (known as the "spine line") has since become commonplace among British lifestyle magazines, including *Q*'s sister publication *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* and the football monthly *[FourFourTwo](/wiki/FourFourTwo "FourFourTwo")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The magazine had a relationship with the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival"), producing both a free daily newspaper on-site during the festival and a review magazine available at the end of the event. That was first started as a *[Select](/wiki/Select_(magazine) "Select (magazine)")* magazine spin-off, although as *Q* moved its focus to the [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") and [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") stars of the 1990s, it was decided that EMAP did not need two monthly titles (and *[Raw](/wiki/Raw_(music_magazine) "Raw (music magazine)")* magazine as well) covering the same genre of music; *Select* was shut in late 2000, with *Q* continuing. In January 2008, *[Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(magazine) "Mojo (magazine)")* was launched by EMAP as a rival to *[Uncut](/wiki/Uncut_(magazine) "Uncut (magazine)")* and focused on all the rock stars, now viewed upon as being heritage and classic, that *Q* originally featured in its pages in 1986. In late 2008, *Q* revamped its image with a smaller amount of text and an increased focus on subjects other than music.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In February 2012, [Andrew Harrison](/wiki/Andrew_Harrison_(journalist) "Andrew Harrison (journalist)") was recruited as editor, replacing Paul Rees during a difficult period when on-line publishing had led to a 17% decline in the magazine's circulation in the first half of 2012. It had fallen to 64,596 units; a reduction in volume described by *The Guardian* as "the worst performance of any music magazine in the period".[[18]](#cite_note-Qe-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Directly reporting to Publishing Director Rimi Atwal of [Bauer Media Group](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Group "Bauer Media Group"), Harrison's brief said to "refocus" and revive the magazine and to that end he took on a number of new journalists launching the magazine's iPad edition, but decided against a rebranding. Under his tenure, *Q* was named "Magazine of the Year" at the 2012 "Record of the Day" awards.[[20]](#cite_note-20) He left just 14 months later, according to *the Guardian*, "as print music magazines continue to endure torrid times" and even free titles were failing to compete against blogs and platforms dependent on [online advertising](/wiki/Online_advertising "Online advertising").[[18]](#cite_note-Qe-18)

In July 2020, Bauer published a Special Collector's Issue of the magazine (Q414), which it had intended to be the last edition[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) before deciding to attempt to sell the publication to another media group. This issue was more of a 'throwback' publication, similar to what *[Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_(magazine) "Mojo (magazine)")* had been doing, and featured articles and acts from 34 years of *Q* magazine. With other firms, such as Long Live Vinyl's owner Anthem Publishing,[[23]](#cite_note-23) ending the publication of a number of monthly music magazine titles, a buyer could not be found for the title, with editor Ted Kessler announcing that issue Q415 would be the last, on 20 July 2020.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[16]](#cite_note-petridis-16)

## Notable articles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable articles")]

In the early days of publication, the magazine's format was much closer in tone to that of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* (though with some of the characteristic humour of former *[Smash Hits](/wiki/Smash_Hits "Smash Hits")* staff shining through), with [Tom Hibbert](/wiki/Tom_Hibbert "Tom Hibbert")'s "Who The Hell..." feature (including interviews with people like [Jeffrey Archer](/wiki/Jeffrey_Archer "Jeffrey Archer"), [Robert Maxwell](/wiki/Robert_Maxwell "Robert Maxwell"), [Ronnie Biggs](/wiki/Ronnie_Biggs "Ronnie Biggs")[[25]](#cite_note-newstatesman.com-25) and [Bernard Manning](/wiki/Bernard_Manning "Bernard Manning")) and film reviews.[[26]](#cite_note-26) After EMAP started to publish a new magazine called *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* in 1989 (the idea being that *Empire* would be 'Q with films'), the movie reviews migrated to the new publication, with *Q* becoming a magazine focused on music (one found for sale alongside *Select* and *[Vox](/wiki/Vox_(magazine) "Vox (magazine)")* in various magazine racks).

In the 1990s, former *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* staff writers, such as [Andrew Collins](/wiki/Andrew_Collins_(broadcaster) "Andrew Collins (broadcaster)"), [Danny Kelly](/wiki/Danny_Kelly_(journalist) "Danny Kelly (journalist)"), [Stuart Maconie](/wiki/Stuart_Maconie "Stuart Maconie"), and [Charles Shaar Murray](/wiki/Charles_Shaar_Murray "Charles Shaar Murray") joined [Paul Du Noyer](/wiki/Paul_Du_Noyer "Paul Du Noyer") and Adrian Deevoy at *Q*. Music coverage in IPC's 'inkie' indie weekly[[27]](#cite_note-27) was becoming more serious after *[Melody Maker](/wiki/Melody_Maker "Melody Maker")* closed down and so writers like Maconie[[28]](#cite_note-28) felt more at home at a publication that would still run [tongue-in-cheek](/wiki/Tongue-in-cheek "Tongue-in-cheek") articles such as "40 Celebs About Whom We Only Know One Thing" and "Do I Have To Wear This, Boss?" (Du Noyer's feature about every band having a member who looks out of place in the line-up).[[25]](#cite_note-newstatesman.com-25)

In 2006, *Q* published a readers' survey, "The 100 Greatest Songs Ever", which was topped by [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)")' "[Live Forever](/wiki/Live_Forever_(Oasis_song) "Live Forever (Oasis song)")".[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the April 2007 issue, *Q* published an article listing "The 100 Greatest Singers", which was topped by [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley").[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga") posed topless in a shoot for the April 2010 issue of the magazine, which was banned by stores in the United States due to the singer revealing too much of her breasts.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Other Q brands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other Q brands")]

After a few years as a radio [jukebox](/wiki/Jukebox "Jukebox"), [Q Radio](/wiki/Q_Radio "Q Radio") launched in June 2008 as a full-service [radio station](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") with a complete roster. Shows and presenters include *Drivetime with Danielle Perry* and *Q the 80s with Matthew Rudd*. The station was transmitted on the [digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") networks in the UK and online. [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay") were involved with the launch of the station by giving an exclusive interview on Q's flagship programme *QPM* on the launch day. It was based in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") alongside the now-closed [Kerrang! 105.2](/wiki/Kerrang!_105.2 "Kerrang! 105.2") after moving from London in 2009. The station was closed in mid-2013 after owners [Bauer Media](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Group "Bauer Media Group") decided to use the station's bandwidth on various platforms (DAB, Digital TV) to launch [Kisstory](/wiki/Kisstory "Kisstory"), a spinoff of their [Kiss](/wiki/Kiss_(Radio_Station) "Kiss (Radio Station)") brand. There was a *[Q TV](/wiki/Q_TV "Q TV")* [television channel](/wiki/Television_channel "Television channel") in the UK, which launched on 2 October 2000 and closed on 3 July 2012.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

*Q* held a yearly awards ceremony called the [Q Awards](/wiki/Q_Awards "Q Awards") from 1990 until 2019. The Q Awards came to an end along with the publication itself.

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(magazine)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Criticism")]

According to the global business magazine *[Campaign](/wiki/Campaign_(magazine) "Campaign (magazine)")* in 2008, *Q* had been criticised for "playing it safe" with its album reviews and cover mounts.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In its early years it was sneered at as "uncool and lacking edge", with [Steven Wells](/wiki/Steven_Wells "Steven Wells") from *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* calling it "the magazine that says 'Hey kids, it's alright to like [Dire Straits](/wiki/Dire_Straits "Dire Straits")'".[[16]](#cite_note-petridis-16)

In a 2001 interview in *[Classic Rock](/wiki/Classic_Rock_(magazine) "Classic Rock (magazine)")*, [Marillion](/wiki/Marillion "Marillion") singer [Steve Hogarth](/wiki/Steve_Hogarth "Steve Hogarth") criticised *Q*'s refusal to cover the band despite publishing some positive reviews.[[34]](#cite_note-Ling2001-34) In 2005, after winning the Q Legend award at the Q Awards, [New Order](/wiki/New_Order_(band) "New Order (band)") bassist [Peter Hook](/wiki/Peter_Hook "Peter Hook") criticised the magazine for being "two-faced" as it had given New Order bad reviews.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
